prompt: Why do greenhouse gases tend to warm the lower atmosphere?
options: They block incoming sunlight completely
They absorb and re-emit outgoing infrared energy
They directly convert sunlight into oxygen
They prevent any heat from leaving Earth

prompt: Which option is best described as removing CO2 from the atmosphere?
options: Burning fossil fuels
Forests and oceans absorbing CO2
Vehicle exhaust emissions
Deforestation releasing stored carbon

prompt: Renewable energy differs from fossil fuels largely because…
options: Renewables have short replenishment timescales compared with fossil fuels
Renewables have higher operating emissions than fossil fuels in every case
Renewables never affect climate
Fossil fuels replenish quickly

prompt: Which process is part of the water cycle?
options: Evaporation and precipitation moving water between atmosphere and surface
Condensation creating additional sunlight
Fusion turning water into oxygen immediately
Only pumps without natural cycling

prompt: A feedback system is most likely if…
options: The effect cannot influence the original cause again
The effect can loop back (directly or indirectly) to influence the original cause
Time lags are irrelevant
No intermediate variables exist

prompt: In a cause-effect chain, an essential practice is to…
options: Skip intermediate variables and jump directly to outcomes
Track the links step-by-step between variables
Assume the relationship is random each time
Focus on only one factor and ignore the rest
A feedback loop where warming can reduce ice, lowering reflectivity and amplifying warming
A purely linear effect with no loop-back
A delay-only process
A relationship unrelated to climate

prompt: Delays in climate systems suggest that…
options: Present actions won’t affect future outcomes
Outcomes may appear years/decades after the initial change
Feedback loops cannot occur
Only short-term impacts count

prompt: Which option correctly describes system interactions?
options: Different parts can affect each other (e.g., energy use can change water reliability)
Parts never influence each other in real systems
Interactions only affect cost, not resources
Interactions are always one-way
Crossing a threshold can shift system behavior more abruptly
The system always changes smoothly without major shifts
Long-term consequences never persist
Thresholds make reversal instantaneous

prompt: Which statement is a reasonable example of sustainability trade-offs?
options: Improving one goal can create challenges for another (e.g., cost or fairness)
There are no trade-offs in sustainability decisions
Only short-term outcomes matter
Fairness is unrelated to sustainability

prompt: If CO2 increases, a best next step in a simple chain is…
options: More CO2 strengthens the greenhouse effect → warming
CO2 instantly turns into oxygen with no warming
Warming reduces CO2 immediately back to zero
CO2 has no effect on greenhouse effect
A change can reinforce itself by altering conditions that cause more change
Each change cancels itself perfectly every step
There is no loop-back in feedback
Outcomes cannot be influenced

prompt: Which mitigation timing better matches systems thinking?
options: Wait until impacts appear, since delays are unavoidable
Act early because feedbacks and delays can increase long-term risk
Mitigation timing cannot change threshold risk
Delaying always reduces cumulative risk

prompt: A systems approach to sustainability planning emphasizes…
options: Feedbacks, delays, and interactions that change outcomes over time
A single direct link with no indirect effects
Ignoring unintended consequences
Only short-term metrics
